sections.title: Use
rows: To say that one person, thing or action differs from another

The city centre is **more crowded than** the suburbs.
Could you speak a little **more slowly**, please?

sections.title: Form: adjectives
rows: one syllable ending in vowel + consonant

sections.title: Form: adverbs
rows: well → **better**, badly → **worse**, early → **earlier**, near → **nearer**, late → **later**, fast → **faster**, far → **farther/further**

sections.title: Useful notes
items: The comparative is usually followed by **than**: *This route is **longer than** the one we took before.*

sections.title: Use
rows: To identify the one member of a group that has the most or least of a quality

This is **the most popular** café in the whole city.
Of all the runners, she finished **the fastest**.

sections.title: Form: adjectives
rows: one syllable ending in vowel + consonant

sections.title: Form: adverbs
rows: well → **best**, badly → **worst**, early → **earliest**, near → **nearest**, late → **latest**, fast → **fastest**, far → **farthest/furthest**

sections.title: Useful notes
items: We normally use **the** before a superlative: *That was **the most memorable** concert I've ever been to.*

exercises.title: A: Complete with comparative adjectives
exercises.instructions: Use the correct comparative form of the adjective in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the correct comparative form of the adjective in brackets.

items.sentence: This chair is ...... (comfortable) than the old one.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is long adjective, so the correct answer is "more comfortable".

wrongOptions.comfortable: The bare infinitive (comfortable) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives compare two things (-er or more + adjective).

items.sentence: My cousin is ...... (young) than me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is short adjective, so the correct answer is "younger".

wrongOptions.young: The bare infinitive (young) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (young) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Travelling by train is ...... (slow) than flying.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is short adjective, so the correct answer is "slower".

wrongOptions.slow: The bare infinitive (slow) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (slow) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Today feels ...... (cold) than yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is short adjective, so the correct answer is "colder".

wrongOptions.cold: The bare infinitive (cold) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: That documentary was ...... (boring) than I expected.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is long adjective, so the correct answer is "more boring".

wrongOptions.boring: The bare infinitive (boring) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives compare two things (-er or more + adjective).

items.sentence: This restaurant is ...... (good) than the one near the station.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is irregular, so the correct answer is "better".

wrongOptions.good: The bare infinitive (good) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

items.sentence: My headache is ...... (bad) today than it was yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is irregular, so the correct answer is "worse".

wrongOptions.bad: The bare infinitive (bad) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The supermarket is ...... (far) away than the pharmacy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is distance, so the correct answer is "farther".

wrongOptions.far: The bare infinitive (far) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (far) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

exercises.studentInstruction: Use the correct superlative form of the adjective in brackets.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Superlatives compare one thing with all others in a group (the most / the -est).

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Superlatives compare one thing with all others in a group (the most / the -est).

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Comparatives and superlatives of adverbs
exercises.title: C: Comparatives and superlatives of adverbs
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form.

items.sentence: Ella dances ...... (gracefully) than her sister.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb comparative, so the correct answer is "more gracefully".

wrongOptions.gracefully: The bare infinitive (gracefully) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives compare two things (-er or more + adjective).

items.sentence: Out of everyone in the race, Josh ran ...... (fast).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative adverb, so the correct answer is "the fastest".

wrongOptions.fast: The bare infinitive (fast) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: My dad drives ...... (carefully) than my uncle.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb, so the correct answer is "more carefully".

wrongOptions.carefully: The bare infinitive (carefully) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives compare two things (-er or more + adjective).

items.sentence: Who arrived ...... (late) yesterday?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative, so the correct answer is "the latest".

wrongOptions.late: The bare infinitive (late) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: A finished past-time expression usually requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

items.sentence: I played ...... (badly) in the second match.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is irregular, so the correct answer is "worse".

wrongOptions.badly: The bare infinitive (badly) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative adverb, so the correct answer is "the best".

wrongOptions.well: The bare infinitive (well) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Superlatives compare one thing with all others in a group (the most / the -est).

items.sentence: Could you walk a little ...... (slowly)?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is comparative adverb, so the correct answer is "more slowly".

wrongOptions.slowly: The bare infinitive (slowly) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives compare two things (-er or more + adjective).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is comparative adverb, so the correct answer is "more quickly".

wrongOptions.quickly: The bare infinitive (quickly) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

exercises.title: D: Choose the correct option
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.originalSentence: This puzzle is **more difficult / difficulter** than the last one.
items.completedSentence: This puzzle is more difficult than the last one.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "more difficult" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "more difficult" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "difficulter" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.difficulter: "difficulter" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("than the last one.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The Amazon is **the longer / the longest** river in South America.
items.completedSentence: The Amazon is the longest river in South America.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "the longest" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the longest" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "the longer" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.the longer: "the longer" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("river in South America.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Your handwriting is **clearer / the clearest** than mine.
items.completedSentence: Your handwriting is clearer than mine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "clearer" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "clearer" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "the clearest" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.the clearest: "the clearest" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("than mine.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Today is **the wetter / the wettest** day this week.
items.completedSentence: Today is the wettest day this week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "the wettest" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the wettest" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "the wetter" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.the wetter: "the wetter" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("day this week.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: This route is **less expensive / the less expensive** than travelling by plane.
items.completedSentence: This route is less expensive than travelling by plane.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "less expensive" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "less expensive" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "the less expensive" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.the less expensive: "the less expensive" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("than travelling by plane.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: She completed the exercise **more carefully / the most carefully** than anyone else.
items.completedSentence: She completed the exercise more carefully than anyone else.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "more carefully" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "more carefully" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "the most carefully" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.the most carefully: "the most carefully" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("than anyone else.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Leo is **the funniest / funnier** student in the class.
items.completedSentence: Leo is the funniest student in the class.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "the funniest" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the funniest" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "funnier" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.funnier: "funnier" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("student in the class.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: That was **the least helpful / less helpful** explanation of all.
items.completedSentence: That was the least helpful explanation of all.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "the least helpful" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the least helpful" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "less helpful" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.less helpful: "less helpful" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("explanation of all.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: E: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake in comparison forms. Find the bold word(s) and write the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake in comparison forms. Find the bold word(s) and write the correct form.

items.sentence: This lesson is boringer than yesterday's.
She is the better organised student in class.
My suitcase is biger than yours.
He studies most carefully than his classmates.
This is the goodest pizza in town.
Today we arrived more late than usual.
Who speaks the better in your family?
It's the most windy afternoon this month.

exercises.title: F: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it means the same.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so it means the same.

items.sentence:
This puzzle is easier than the crossword. **DIFFICULT**
The crossword ...... this puzzle.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is opposite of easier, so the correct answer is "is more difficult than".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is more difficult than", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives and superlatives compare people, things, or actions.

items.sentence:
Nobody in the class speaks as clearly as Emma. **CLEARLY**
Emma ...... in the class.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative, so the correct answer is "speaks the most clearly".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "speaks the most clearly", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
My old bike wasn't as comfortable as this one. **MORE**
This bike ...... my old one.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is comparative, so the correct answer is "is more comfortable than".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is more comfortable than", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Ryan is the strongest player on the team. **THAN**
Ryan is ...... any other player on the team.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is comparative, so the correct answer is "stronger than".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "stronger than", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
This was the least useful app on my phone. **USELESS**
This was ...... app on my phone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is opposite of least useful, so the correct answer is "the most useless".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the most useless", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I drive more carefully than my brother. **CAREFULLY**
My brother doesn't drive ...... me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is equal comparison, so the correct answer is "as carefully as".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "as carefully as", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: G: Mixed comparison practice
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has a mistake in the comparative or superlative form. Write the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has a mistake in the comparative or superlative form. Write the correct form.

items.sentence: This sofa is more soft than the old one.
She is the most young teacher at the school.
My new computer is more fast than my old one.
He swims more well than anyone in the team.
That was the worstest meal I've ever eaten.
This coat is more warm than that one.
My sister is more short than me.
It was the most foggy morning of the year.
